mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that enables security, compliance, automation, and governance through policy-as-code. Installing Kyverno allows you to enforce policies on your Kubernetes resources effectively.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Kyverno Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kyverno using Helm
steps.description:
Next, install Kyverno in your Kubernetes cluster with the following command. This will create the necessary namespace and install Kyverno v1.16.3:
```bash
helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --create-namespace --version 3.6.3
```

**Note:** The chart version is `3.6.3` (app version v1.16.3). Do not use `--version v1.16.3` — that is the app version, not the chart version.

**Version compatibility:** Chart `3.7.1` (v1.16.3) requires Kubernetes 1.32+ due to its dependency on the `MutatingAdmissionPolicy` v1alpha1 API. For Kubernetes 1.25–1.31 (including OpenShift 4.14–4.18), use chart `3.6.3` (v1.16.3).

**OpenShift clusters:** Kyverno pods require Security Context Constraints (SCCs). After install, grant `nonroot-v2` SCC to all Kyverno service accounts:
```bash
for sa in kyverno-admission-controller kyverno-background-controller kyverno-cleanup-controller kyverno-reports-controller; do
  kubectl create clusterrolebinding "kyverno-scc-${sa}" --clusterrole=system:openshift:scc:nonroot-v2 --serviceaccount="kyverno:${sa}"
done
kubectl -n kyverno rollout restart deployment kyverno-admission-controller kyverno-background-controller kyverno-cleanup-controller kyverno-reports-controller
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kyverno pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kyverno
```

steps.title: Configure Kyverno policies
steps.description:
After installation, you can configure Kyverno policies. For example, to set the Pod Security Standard to 'baseline', you can modify the `values.yaml` file or set it directly in the Helm command:
```bash
helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --set podSecurityStandard=baseline
```

steps.title: Test a sample policy
steps.description:
To ensure Kyverno is working as expected, apply a sample audit policy. **Important:** Use `validationFailureAction: Audit` (not `Enforce`) to avoid breaking workloads during initial testing.

Create a YAML file named `sample-policy.yaml` and apply it:
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
    - name: check-team-label
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "The label 'team' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              team: "?*"
```
Then apply it:
```bash
kubectl apply -f sample-policy.yaml
kubectl get clusterpolicy
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kyverno will show the Kyverno pods in a running state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace kyverno`. You can also verify the configuration by applying a sample policy and checking for enforcement.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno
helm repo update
helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --set podSecurityStandard=baseline

apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-non-root
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: require-non-root
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Run as non-root user"
        pattern:
          spec:
            securityContext:
              runAsUser: 1000

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Kyverno installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources:
```bash
helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created during the installation. This ensures that no leftover resources remain:
```bash
kubectl delete crd clusterpolicies.kyverno.io
kubectl delete crd policies.kyverno.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the Kyverno namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kyverno
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Kyverno installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kyverno -o yaml > kyverno-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade your Kyverno installation to the desired version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version v1.18.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, check that the Kyverno pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kyverno
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that Kyverno pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more details on the failure:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kyverno
```
Common issues include misconfigured policies or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno policies not applying
troubleshooting.description:
If your policies are not being enforced, verify that the policies are correctly applied and check the status:
```bash
kubectl get cpol --namespace kyverno
kubectl describe cpol <policy-name> --namespace kyverno
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the upgrade, ensure that the version you are trying to upgrade to is compatible with your current installation. You can check the installed version with:
```bash
helm list --namespace kyverno
```

troubleshooting.title: Namespace not found error
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error indicating that the namespace 'kyverno' is not found, ensure that the namespace exists and is correctly specified in your commands:
```bash
kubectl get namespaces
```

troubleshooting.title: Admission controller TLS secret not created (K8s < 1.32)
troubleshooting.description:
If the admission controller pod stays Not Ready with repeated `TLS handshake error: secret kyverno-svc.kyverno.svc.kyverno-tls-pair not found` messages, you are likely running Kyverno chart 3.7.1+ on Kubernetes < 1.32. Chart 3.7.1 (app v1.17.1) requires the `MutatingAdmissionPolicy` v1alpha1 API which is only available in Kubernetes 1.32+. The missing API blocks TLS certificate generation.

**Fix:** Downgrade to chart 3.6.3 (app v1.16.3):
```bash
helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno --no-hooks
kubectl -n kyverno delete secret -l owner=helm,name=kyverno
helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --create-namespace --version 3.6.3
```

troubleshooting.title: Reports controller OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
The Kyverno reports controller defaults to 128Mi memory limit, which may be insufficient for clusters with many workloads. If the pod shows OOMKilled, increase its memory:
```bash
kubectl -n kyverno patch deployment kyverno-reports-controller --type=json -p='[{"op":"replace","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/limits/memory","value":"512Mi"},{"op":"replace","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/requests/memory","value":"256Mi"}]'
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm CLI installed.
